facts.label: THE BLUE THAT PRECEDED GREEN
facts.story: Ireland's very first international shirt wasn't green at all. At the 1924 Paris Olympics, the team's official color clashed with opponents Bulgaria, who also played in green, so Ireland switched to their change strip: a deep St. Patrick's blue. It sat forgotten for nearly a century until 2021, when the FAI revived it for a centenary friendly against Qatar, briefly retiring the emerald shirt that had defined the team for generations.

facts.label: SWITCHING SHIRTS MID-CHASE
facts.story: Ireland's kit supplier changed hands in the middle of the road to a World Cup. After 15 years with Adidas, a relationship struck when Jack Charlton took charge in 1986, the FAI switched to Umbro partway through qualifying for 2002. Players who'd spent a campaign in Adidas Equipment stripes finished it in Umbro green, then wore the new kit all the way to Suwon and a round-of-16 shootout with Spain.

1990.headline: Unbeaten Into the Quarters

annotations.label: BONNER'S SAVE, O'LEARY'S NERVE
annotations.story: Goalless after 120 minutes against Romania in Genoa, the shootout reached sudden death. Packie Bonner dove right to smother Daniel Timofte's tame effort, and up stepped defender David O'Leary — who had never taken a competitive penalty for Ireland — to send his country into its first-ever World Cup quarter-final.

1990.homeColors: emerald green shirt with white and orange wrap-over V-neck trim (Adidas), white shorts, green socks

1994.headline: Houghton Stuns the Italians

annotations.label: ELEVEN MINUTES IN NEW JERSEY
annotations.story: Ray Houghton lobbed Gianluca Pagliuca from 20 yards inside the opening quarter-hour at Giants Stadium, and Ireland's back line held out an Italy team featuring Baggio and Baresi for the remaining 79. It remains one of the great World Cup upsets, and Ireland's only win of the tournament.

1994.homeColors: green shirt with bold contrasting 'Adidas Equipment' panels, white shorts, green socks
1994.awayColors: white shirt with green and orange Adidas Equipment trim

2002.headline: Keane's Stoppage-Time Rescue

annotations.label: ONE MINUTE FROM ELIMINATION
annotations.story: Germany led 1–0 deep into stoppage time in Ibaraki when Niall Quinn flicked on a long ball and Robbie Keane buried it first-time past Oliver Kahn in the 90th minute. The draw kept Ireland's group-stage hopes alive and became the signature moment of Mick McCarthy's tournament.

2002.homeColors: green shirt with white trim (Umbro), white shorts, green socks
